expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of bacterial metabolism—the diverse biochemical strategies bacteria employ to obtain energy and carbon for growth—detailing the four nutritional classifications (photoautotrophs, photoheterotrophs, chemoautotrophs, chemoheterotrophs), central metabolic pathways (glycolysis, pentose phosphate pathway, TCA cycle), respiratory and fermentative energy generation, and specialized metabolic adaptations that enable bacteria to exploit virtually every ecological niche on Earth.

core_concept.principle: Bacterial metabolic diversity encompasses all known strategies for energy generation and carbon acquisition, enabling bacteria to exploit resources ranging from sunlight and inorganic chemicals to complex organic compounds across aerobic and anaerobic environments.
core_concept.explanation: Bacterial metabolism is classified by energy source (light vs. chemical compounds) and carbon source (CO₂ vs. organic compounds), yielding four nutritional types: photoautotrophs (light + CO₂, e.g., Cyanobacteria), photoheterotrophs (light + organic carbon, e.g., purple non-sulfur bacteria), chemoautotrophs (inorganic chemicals + CO₂, e.g., nitrifiers, sulfur oxidizers), and chemoheterotrophs (organic compounds for both energy and carbon, e.g., E. coli, pathogens). Energy generation occurs via respiration (aerobic or anaerobic, using electron transport chains with O₂ or alternative acceptors like NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Fe³⁺) or fermentation (substrate-level phosphorylation without electron transport). Central pathways—glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas), pentose phosphate pathway, and TCA cycle—provide precursors for biosynthesis and feed electrons into respiratory chains. Metabolic flexibility allows bacteria to switch pathways based on environmental conditions (e.g., E. coli uses aerobic respiration when O₂ is available, fermentation when anaerobic). Understanding bacterial metabolism is essential for biotechnology (fermentation, bioremediation), medicine (targeting pathogen metabolism), and ecology (nutrient cycling).

image.alt: Diagram showing four bacterial nutritional types with energy and carbon sources
image.caption: Bacterial nutrition: Photoautotrophs (light + CO₂), photoheterotrophs (light + organic C), chemoautotrophs (inorganic chemicals + CO₂), chemoheterotrophs (organic compounds). Energy via respiration (ETC) or fermentation (substrate-level phosphorylation).

sections.section_title: Introduction: Metabolic Diversity as Bacterial Success Strategy
sections.introduction: Bacteria represent the most metabolically diverse organisms on Earth, capable of exploiting energy and carbon sources inaccessible to eukaryotes. This diversity underpins their ecological dominance: bacteria inhabit deep-sea hydrothermal vents (chemolithotrophs using H₂S), acidic mine drainage (acidophiles oxidizing Fe²⁺), animal guts (fermenters breaking down complex polysaccharides), and sunlit surface waters (photoautotrophs fixing CO₂). The evolution of metabolic pathways reflects ancient innovations: glycolysis likely predates the divergence of Bacteria and Archaea; photosynthesis evolved in ancestors of Cyanobacteria, oxygenating Earth's atmosphere ~2.4 billion years ago; anaerobic respiration pathways enabled survival before atmospheric O₂ accumulation. Bacterial metabolism is not merely a collection of pathways but an integrated, regulated network that responds dynamically to environmental cues. Understanding these strategies reveals how bacteria drive global biogeochemical cycles (C, N, S, Fe), support eukaryotic life through symbioses, cause disease by exploiting host nutrients, and provide tools for biotechnology (enzyme production, bioremediation, biofuels). This section explores bacterial nutritional classifications, central metabolic pathways, respiratory and fermentative energy generation, and specialized adaptations that enable metabolic versatility.

introduction_image.alt: Collage showing bacteria in diverse metabolic niches: hydrothermal vents, soil, gut, surface waters
introduction_image.caption: Metabolic niches: Hydrothermal vents (chemolithotrophs: H₂, H₂S), soil (decomposers: complex organics), gut (fermenters: polysaccharides), surface waters (photoautotrophs: light + CO₂). Metabolic diversity enables global colonization.

key_concept.definition: Bacterial metabolism encompasses the biochemical pathways for energy generation (respiration, fermentation) and carbon acquisition (autotrophy, heterotrophy), classified by energy source (light/chemical) and carbon source (CO₂/organic).
key_concept.context: Metabolic classification reflects ecological strategy: photoautotrophs dominate sunlit environments; chemoautotrophs exploit inorganic energy sources in dark habitats; chemoheterotrophs decompose organic matter or parasitize hosts. Pathway regulation (e.g., catabolite repression, two-component systems) enables rapid adaptation to changing nutrient availability. Metabolic flexibility often involves redundant pathways (e.g., multiple terminal oxidases) ensuring function under variable conditions.

image.alt: Matrix showing bacterial nutritional types by energy and carbon source
image.caption: Nutritional classification: Energy source (light/chemical) × carbon source (CO₂/organic) = four types. Photoautotrophs (Cyanobacteria), chemoautotrophs (nitrifiers), chemoheterotrophs (E. coli), photoheterotrophs (purple non-sulfur bacteria).

sections.key_concept: Bacterial metabolic strategies are organized by energy source (phototrophy vs. chemotrophy) and carbon source (autotrophy vs. heterotrophy), with central pathways (glycolysis, TCA cycle) and terminal processes (respiration, fermentation) determining ecological function.

classifications.type: Phototrophic Metabolism: Light as Energy Source
classifications.value: Best for understanding how bacteria harness light energy for ATP synthesis and carbon fixation
characteristics: Photoautotrophs: Light energy + CO₂ carbon (Cyanobacteria: oxygenic photosynthesis; purple/green bacteria: anoxygenic)
Photoheterotrophs: Light energy + organic carbon (purple non-sulfur bacteria, some proteobacteria)
Photosystems: Type II (P680, oxygenic) in Cyanobacteria; Type I (P700) or bacterial reaction centers (anoxygenic)
Electron donors: H₂O (oxygenic, produces O₂); H₂S, S⁰, H₂, or organic compounds (anoxygenic, no O₂)
Carbon fixation: Calvin cycle (Cyanobacteria, purple bacteria); reverse TCA or 3-hydroxypropionate cycles (some green bacteria)
classifications.behavior: Phototrophic bacteria use light-harvesting complexes (phycobilisomes in Cyanobacteria, chlorosomes in green bacteria) to capture photons and transfer energy to reaction centers. In oxygenic photosynthesis (Cyanobacteria), photosystem II splits H₂O, releasing O₂ and providing electrons to photosystem I for NADPH production; cyclic electron flow generates ATP. Anoxygenic phototrophs use a single photosystem with alternative electron donors (H₂S → S⁰ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻), avoiding O₂ production. Photoheterotrophs use light for ATP via cyclic photophosphorylation but require organic carbon for biosynthesis, thriving in organic-rich, low-O₂ environments. Phototrophs often regulate photosynthesis gene expression via light intensity, O₂ tension, and redox state.

examples.process: Cyanobacterial Oxygenic Photosynthesis and Carbon Fixation
examples.explanation: Cyanobacteria perform oxygenic photosynthesis using two photosystems: (1) PSII (P680) absorbs light, oxidizes H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻; electrons pass through plastoquinone, cytochrome b₆f, plastocyanin to PSI; (2) PSI (P700) re-excites electrons, reducing ferredoxin → NADPH via ferredoxin-NADP⁺ reductase; (3) Proton gradient from electron transport drives ATP synthesis via ATP synthase. ATP and NADPH fuel the Calvin cycle: Rubisco fixes CO₂ to RuBP → 3-PGA → G3P for carbohydrate synthesis. Cyanobacteria also perform cyclic electron flow around PSI to generate additional ATP without NADPH, balancing energy needs. Some fix N₂ via nitrogenase in heterocysts (specialized cells with thick walls excluding O₂).

image.alt: Cyanobacterial thylakoid membrane showing PSII, PSI, electron transport, and ATP synthesis
image.caption: Oxygenic photosynthesis: PSII splits H₂O → O₂ + e⁻; e⁻ flow through ETC → PSI → NADPH; proton gradient → ATP. Calvin cycle in stroma fixes CO₂. Heterocysts fix N₂ anaerobically.

examples.process: Anoxygenic Photosynthesis in Purple Sulfur Bacteria
examples.explanation: Purple sulfur bacteria (e.g., Chromatium) perform anoxygenic photosynthesis using a single Type II reaction center homologous to PSII. Electron donor: H₂S → S⁰ (stored intracellularly) + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻. Electrons cycle through quinones, cytochrome bc₁, and back to reaction center (cyclic photophosphorylation), generating proton gradient for ATP synthesis. NADH is produced via reverse electron flow (energy-dependent reduction of NAD⁺). Carbon fixation uses Calvin cycle (some use reverse TCA). These bacteria thrive in anoxic, sulfide-rich environments (stratified lakes, microbial mats). S⁰ oxidation to SO₄²⁻ provides additional energy via chemolithotrophy when light is limited, demonstrating metabolic flexibility.

image.alt: Purple sulfur bacterial membrane showing cyclic electron flow and H₂S oxidation
image.caption: Anoxygenic photosynthesis: Single reaction center; H₂S → S⁰ + 2e⁻; cyclic e⁻ flow → proton gradient → ATP. Reverse e⁻ flow produces NADH. Calvin cycle fixes CO₂. S⁰ stored intracellularly.

classifications.type: Chemotrophic Metabolism: Chemical Compounds as Energy Source
classifications.value: Best for understanding how bacteria extract energy from inorganic or organic chemicals via respiration or fermentation
characteristics: Chemoautotrophs (lithotrophs): Inorganic electron donors (H₂, NH₃, H₂S, Fe²⁺) + CO₂ carbon; often aerobic but some anaerobic
Chemoheterotrophs (organotrophs): Organic compounds for both energy and carbon; most pathogens, decomposers, gut symbionts
Respiration: Electron transport chain with terminal acceptor (O₂, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Fe³⁺, CO₂); generates proton motive force for ATP
Fermentation: Substrate-level phosphorylation without ETC; organic compound serves as both electron donor and acceptor
Metabolic flexibility: Many bacteria switch between respiration and fermentation based on O₂ availability (facultative anaerobes)
classifications.behavior: Chemoautotrophs oxidize inorganic compounds to generate reducing power (NADH) and proton motive force. Examples: nitrifiers (Nitrosomonas: NH₃ → NO₂⁻; Nitrobacter: NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻); sulfur oxidizers (Thiobacillus: H₂S → SO₄²⁻); hydrogen bacteria (Ralstonia: H₂ → H⁺); iron oxidizers (Acidithiobacillus: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺). Electrons enter ETC at various points, generating proton gradient for ATP; reverse electron flow may be needed to reduce NAD⁺. Chemoheterotrophs catabolize organic compounds via glycolysis, TCA cycle, and ETC (aerobic) or fermentation (anaerobic). Facultative anaerobes (E. coli) preferentially use aerobic respiration (higher ATP yield) but switch to fermentation or anaerobic respiration when O₂ is limited. Obligate anaerobes (Clostridium) lack SOD/catalase and are killed by O₂.

examples.explanation: Nitrification converts NH₃ to NO₃⁻ via two bacterial groups: (1) Ammonia oxidizers (Nitrosomonas, Nitrosococcus): NH₃ + O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → NH₂OH + H₂O (ammonia monooxygenase, AMO); NH₂OH + H₂O → NO₂⁻ + 5H⁺ + 4e⁻ (hydroxylamine oxidoreductase, HAO). Electrons enter ETC, generating proton motive force; reverse electron flow produces NADH for CO₂ fixation via Calvin cycle. (2) Nitrite oxidizers (Nitrobacter, Nitrospira): NO₂⁻ + H₂O → NO₃⁻ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ (nitrite oxidoreductase, NXR). Electrons feed ETC for ATP; NADH via reverse flow. Nitrification is aerobic, pH-sensitive (optimal pH 7-8), and critical for nitrogen availability in soils and wastewater treatment.

image.alt: Nitrification pathway showing ammonia and nitrite oxidation steps with enzyme complexes
image.caption: Nitrification: Ammonia oxidizers (AMO, HAO) convert NH₃ → NO₂⁻; nitrite oxidizers (NXR) convert NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻. Electrons feed ETC for ATP; reverse flow produces NADH for CO₂ fixation.

examples.explanation: E. coli performs mixed-acid fermentation under anaerobic conditions when no external electron acceptor is available. Glycolysis converts glucose to 2 pyruvate + 2 ATP + 2 NADH. Pyruvate is diverted to multiple products to regenerate NAD⁺: (1) Lactate dehydrogenase: pyruvate + NADH → lactate + NAD⁺; (2) Pyruvate formate-lyase: pyruvate → acetyl-CoA + formate; (3) Acetyl-CoA → acetate + ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation) or → ethanol + CO₂; (4) Formate → H₂ + CO₂ (formate hydrogenlyase). Product ratios depend on pH, growth rate, and strain. Fermentation yields only 2 ATP/glucose vs. ~38 ATP via aerobic respiration, but enables growth without O₂ or alternative acceptors.
examples.equation: $\text{Glucose fermentation: } \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow \text{mixture of lactate, acetate, ethanol, formate, H}_2, \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{ATP}$

image.alt: Mixed-acid fermentation pathway in E. coli showing pyruvate branch points
image.caption: Mixed-acid fermentation: Glucose → 2 pyruvate (glycolysis, 2 ATP) → lactate, acetate, ethanol, formate, H₂/CO₂. NAD⁺ regeneration enables continued glycolysis. Low ATP yield but O₂-independent.

classifications.value: Best for understanding energy generation in anoxic environments using terminal acceptors other than O₂
characteristics: Denitrification: NO₃⁻ → NO₂⁻ → NO → N₂O → N₂ (Paracoccus, Pseudomonas); important in nitrogen cycling
Sulfate reduction: SO₄²⁻ → H₂S (Desulfovibrio); dominates anoxic marine sediments
Iron reduction: Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ (Geobacter, Shewanella); important in soil/aquatic Fe cycling
Methanogenesis: CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ (Archaea, not Bacteria); strict anaerobes in wetlands, guts
Acetogenesis: CO₂ + 4H₂ → acetate (Acetobacterium); syntrophic partnerships with methanogens
classifications.behavior: Anaerobic respiration uses electron transport chains with terminal reductases specific to alternative acceptors. Energy yield depends on redox potential difference between donor and acceptor: O₂/H₂O (+820 mV) yields most ATP; NO₃⁻/N₂ (+740 mV) yields ~90% of aerobic; SO₄²⁻/H₂S (-220 mV) yields much less. Anaerobic respirers often have multiple terminal reductases, switching based on acceptor availability (e.g., E. coli uses NO₃⁻ reductase when O₂ is absent). Some anaerobes couple respiration to proton pumping (e.g., nitrate reductase complexes); others rely on substrate-level phosphorylation. Anaerobic respiration drives biogeochemical cycles in anoxic zones (sediments, water columns, guts) and enables bioremediation of pollutants (e.g., dechlorination via reductive dehalogenation).

examples.process: Denitrification: Stepwise Nitrate Reduction to N₂
examples.explanation: Denitrification reduces NO₃⁻ to N₂ via four enzymatic steps, each catalyzed by a distinct reductase: (1) Nitrate reductase (Nar): NO₃⁻ + 2e⁻ + 2H⁺ → NO₂⁻ + H₂O; (2) Nitrite reductase (Nir): NO₂⁻ + e⁻ + 2H⁺ → NO + H₂O; (3) Nitric oxide reductase (Nor): 2NO + 2e⁻ + 2H⁺ → N₂O + H₂O; (4) Nitrous oxide reductase (Nos): N₂O + 2e⁻ + 2H⁺ → N₂ + H₂O. Electrons from organic donors (e.g., succinate) or H₂ enter ETC, generating proton motive force for ATP synthesis. Denitrification occurs under low O₂ (reductases are O₂-sensitive) and is regulated by Fnr, NarXL two-component systems. Ecological impact: returns fixed N to atmosphere, completing N cycle; N₂O is a potent greenhouse gas. Biotechnological use: wastewater treatment removes NO₃⁻ via denitrification.

image.alt: Denitrification pathway showing four reductase steps from NO₃⁻ to N₂
image.caption: Denitrification: Nar (NO₃⁻→NO₂⁻) → Nir (NO₂⁻→NO) → Nor (NO→N₂O) → Nos (N₂O→N₂). Electrons from organic donors/H₂ feed ETC for ATP. Regulated by O₂ tension and NO₃⁻ availability.

examples.explanation: Geobacter sulfurreducens reduces Fe³⁺ (insoluble oxides) to Fe²⁺ using organic acids (acetate) or H₂ as electron donors. Mechanism: (1) Acetate oxidation via TCA cycle generates electrons; (2) Electrons pass through inner membrane ETC (quinones, cytochromes) to periplasmic cytochromes; (3) Outer membrane cytochromes (OmcS, OmcZ) and conductive pili ('microbial nanowires') transfer electrons to extracellular Fe³⁺ oxides. Proton motive force from ETC drives ATP synthesis. Fe³⁺ reduction is coupled to proton pumping at some steps, enhancing energy yield. Ecological role: Fe³⁺ reduction dominates anoxic soil/sediment metabolism, influencing Fe, C, and contaminant (e.g., uranium) mobility. Biotechnological applications: bioremediation of metals/radionuclides; microbial fuel cells generate electricity from organic waste.

image.alt: Geobacter electron transfer pathway from acetate oxidation to extracellular Fe³⁺ reduction
image.caption: Iron reduction: Acetate → TCA cycle → inner membrane ETC → periplasmic cytochromes → outer membrane cytochromes/pili → extracellular Fe³⁺. Conductive pili enable long-range electron transfer to insoluble acceptors.

principles.principle: Metabolic Pathways Are Organized to Balance Energy Generation, Precursor Supply, and Redox Homeostasis
principles.statement: Central metabolic pathways (glycolysis, pentose phosphate, TCA cycle) serve dual roles: generating ATP/NAD(P)H and providing biosynthetic precursors, with flux regulated to match cellular demands.
principles.application: Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas pathway) converts glucose to pyruvate, yielding 2 ATP (substrate-level) and 2 NADH; intermediates feed amino acid, lipid, and nucleotide synthesis. Pentose phosphate pathway generates NADPH (for biosynthesis, oxidative stress defense) and pentose sugars (for nucleotides). TCA cycle oxidizes acetyl-CoA to CO₂, producing NADH, FADH₂ (for ETC), and precursors (α-ketoglutarate for amino acids, oxaloacetate for aspartate). Flux is regulated by allosteric effectors (e.g., ATP inhibits phosphofructokinase in glycolysis; NADH inhibits isocitrate dehydrogenase in TCA) and transcriptional control (e.g., ArcAB regulates TCA genes in response to redox state). This integration ensures efficient resource use: excess carbon is stored as glycogen or polyhydroxyalkanoates; limiting nutrients trigger scavenging systems.
principles.example: E. coli metabolic flexibility: In glucose-rich, aerobic conditions, glycolysis and TCA cycle operate at high flux, generating ATP and precursors for rapid growth. When glucose is depleted, cAMP-CRP activates genes for alternative carbon sources (lactose, acetate). Under anaerobiosis, ArcA represses TCA cycle enzymes, favoring fermentation or anaerobic respiration. This regulatory network optimizes growth across environments, illustrating how pathway integration supports metabolic versatility.
principles.analogy: Central metabolism is like a city's utility grid: glycolysis is the main power plant (ATP generation); pentose phosphate is the specialty workshop (NADPH, pentoses); TCA cycle is the recycling center (precursors, more ATP). Traffic controllers (allosteric regulators, transcription factors) direct flow based on demand (growth rate) and supply (nutrient availability), preventing blackouts (energy deficit) or waste (excess intermediates).

image.alt: Diagram showing glycolysis, pentose phosphate, and TCA cycle with interconnected precursors and regulation
image.caption: Central metabolism: Glycolysis (ATP, NADH, precursors), pentose phosphate (NADPH, pentoses), TCA cycle (NADH, FADH₂, precursors). Allosteric/transcriptional regulation balances energy, redox, and biosynthesis.

principles.principle: Electron Transport Chains Maximize Energy Yield via Proton Motive Force Generation
principles.statement: Respiratory electron transport chains couple electron flow from donors to acceptors with proton pumping across membranes, generating proton motive force for ATP synthesis and transport.
principles.application: Bacterial ETCs are modular: dehydrogenases (e.g., NADH dehydrogenase, succinate dehydrogenase) oxidize donors, passing electrons to quinones (ubiquinone, menaquinone); quinones shuttle electrons to terminal reductases (e.g., cytochrome oxidases for O₂, nitrate reductase for NO₃⁻). Proton pumping occurs at specific complexes (e.g., Complex I homologs pump 4 H⁺/2e⁻; cytochrome bc₁ pumps 2 H⁺/2e⁻), creating Δp (Δψ + ΔpH) that drives ATP synthase (3-4 H⁺/ATP) and solute transport. Bacteria often have multiple, branched ETCs: E. coli has two NADH dehydrogenases (NDH-1 pumps protons; NDH-2 does not), four terminal oxidases with different O₂ affinities, enabling function across O₂ gradients. This modularity allows adaptation to varying donor/acceptor availability while optimizing energy yield.
principles.example: E. coli aerobic respiration: NADH donates electrons to NDH-1 (pumps 4 H⁺) → ubiquinone → cytochrome bo₃ oxidase (pumps 2 H⁺, consumes 2 H⁺ in periplasm for O₂ reduction) → H₂O. Net: ~10 H⁺ pumped per NADH, yielding ~2.5-3 ATP. Under low O₂, cytochrome bd oxidase (higher O₂ affinity, no proton pumping) is expressed, sacrificing yield for function. This trade-off illustrates how ETC flexibility balances energy efficiency with environmental adaptation.
principles.analogy: ETCs are like hydroelectric dams: electron flow is water cascading downhill; proton pumping is lifting water to a reservoir (proton gradient); ATP synthase is the turbine generating power. Multiple dams (ETC branches) allow operation at different water levels (O₂ concentrations), ensuring continuous power generation even when conditions change.

image.alt: Modular bacterial electron transport chain showing dehydrogenases, quinones, and terminal reductases
image.caption: Bacterial ETC: Dehydrogenases (NADH, succinate) → quinones (UQ, MQ) → terminal reductases (O₂, NO₃⁻, etc.). Proton pumping at specific complexes generates Δp for ATP synthesis. Branched chains enable adaptation to varying conditions.

principles.principle: Metabolic Regulation Coordinates Pathway Activity with Environmental Cues and Cellular Demands
principles.statement: Bacteria use allosteric regulation, covalent modification, and transcriptional control to adjust metabolic flux in response to nutrient availability, energy status, and stress.
principles.application: Key regulatory mechanisms: (1) Allosteric control: ATP inhibits phosphofructokinase (glycolysis); citrate inhibits phosphofructokinase and activates fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (gluconeogenesis); (2) Covalent modification: Iso-citrate dehydrogenase is inactivated by phosphorylation during growth on acetate, redirecting flux to glyoxylate shunt; (3) Transcriptional control: Catabolite repression (cAMP-CRP) prioritizes preferred carbon sources; two-component systems (e.g., NarXL) induce anaerobic respiration genes when O₂ is low; (4) Small RNAs: RyhB sRNA represses iron-using enzymes under Fe limitation, prioritizing essential functions. This multi-layered regulation ensures efficient resource allocation: rapid responses (allostery) adjust flux within seconds; transcriptional changes (minutes-hours) remodel metabolism for sustained conditions.
principles.example: Catabolite repression in E. coli: When glucose is present, low cAMP levels prevent CRP activation, repressing genes for alternative carbon sources (lactose, arabinose). When glucose is depleted, cAMP rises, CRP activates alternative catabolic operons. Simultaneously, PTS system phosphorylation state signals glucose availability, regulating enzyme activity (e.g., inhibiting non-PTS sugar transporters). This hierarchical control ensures preferential use of high-yield substrates, optimizing growth rate—a key fitness trait in competitive environments.
principles.analogy: Metabolic regulation is like a smart home system: allosteric controls are motion sensors (instant light adjustments); covalent modifications are programmable thermostats (scheduled changes); transcriptional control is the central hub (long-term planning); sRNAs are voice commands (quick overrides). Together, they maintain comfort (growth) while minimizing energy waste (resource inefficiency).

image.alt: Multi-layered metabolic regulation showing allosteric, covalent, transcriptional, and sRNA control
image.caption: Regulation layers: Allosteric (seconds, enzyme activity), covalent (minutes, enzyme modification), transcriptional (minutes-hours, gene expression), sRNA (rapid, post-transcriptional). Integrated control matches metabolism to environment and demand.

sections.section_title: Key Components of Bacterial Metabolic Pathways

components.definition: Bacterial metabolism integrates catabolic pathways (energy generation), anabolic pathways (biosynthesis), and regulatory networks, with central metabolism providing precursors and energy for cellular functions.
components.equation: $\text{Metabolic flux} = f(\text{substrate availability}, \text{enzyme activity}, \text{regulatory signals}, \text{energy/redox status})$
components.interpretation: Metabolic efficiency depends on coordinated pathway operation: catabolism generates ATP/NAD(P)H and precursors; anabolism consumes these for biomass synthesis; regulation balances supply and demand. Central pathways (glycolysis, TCA) are highly conserved, while peripheral pathways (specialized catabolism, secondary metabolism) vary widely, reflecting ecological adaptation. Understanding component interactions enables metabolic engineering for biotechnology (e.g., optimizing product yields) and antimicrobial strategies (targeting pathogen-specific pathways).

image.alt: Integrated diagram of bacterial catabolism, anabolism, and regulation
image.caption: Metabolic integration: Catabolism (glycolysis, respiration) → ATP/NAD(P)H + precursors; Anabolism (biosynthesis) → biomass; Regulation (allosteric, transcriptional) → flux control. Central pathways conserved; peripheral pathways adapted to niche.

categories.type: Glycolysis and Alternative Pathways: Glucose Catabolism Strategies
categories.description: Bacteria employ multiple pathways for glucose catabolism, with glycolysis (EMP) most common but alternatives (ED, PK) prevalent in specific taxa or conditions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Three major glucose catabolic pathways: (1) Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP): 10-step pathway yielding 2 ATP, 2 NADH, 2 pyruvate per glucose; universal in bacteria, regulated at phosphofructokinase; (2) Entner-Doudoroff (ED): 6-step pathway yielding 1 ATP, 1 NADH, 1 NADPH, 2 pyruvate; common in Pseudomonas, Zymomonas; lower ATP yield but generates NADPH directly; (3) Pentose phosphate (PP): oxidative branch generates NADPH and pentoses; non-oxidative branch interconverts sugars. Pathway choice reflects ecological strategy: EMP maximizes ATP for fast growth; ED provides NADPH for biosynthesis in oligotrophs; PP supports nucleotide synthesis and oxidative stress defense. Some bacteria use multiple pathways simultaneously, with flux partitioned based on demand.

examples.explanation: P. putida, a soil bacterium, uses the ED pathway as its primary glucose catabolic route. Mechanism: Glucose → glucose-6-P → 6-phosphogluconate → KDPG (2-keto-3-deoxy-6-phosphogluconate) → pyruvate + glyceraldehyde-3-P. KDPG aldolase cleaves KDPG, a unique ED enzyme. Net yield: 1 ATP, 1 NADH, 1 NADPH per glucose (vs. EMP: 2 ATP, 2 NADH). Advantages: (1) Shorter pathway (fewer enzymes, lower biosynthetic cost); (2) Direct NADPH production for biosynthesis/antioxidant defense; (3) KDPG is a signaling molecule regulating virulence in some pathogens. P. putida's preference for ED reflects its oligotrophic lifestyle: efficient NADPH generation supports biosynthesis under nutrient limitation, while lower ATP yield is tolerable in slow-growth conditions.

image.alt: Entner-Doudoroff pathway showing key enzymes and cofactor yields
image.caption: ED pathway: Glucose → 6-P-gluconate → KDPG (ED-specific) → pyruvate + G3P. Yields 1 ATP, 1 NADH, 1 NADPH. Common in Pseudomonas; favors NADPH production for biosynthesis under nutrient limitation.

categories.type: Anaplerotic Reactions and the Glyoxylate Shunt: Balancing Catabolism and Anabolism
categories.description: Anaplerotic reactions replenish TCA cycle intermediates consumed for biosynthesis; the glyoxylate shunt enables growth on two-carbon compounds by bypassing CO₂-generating steps
categories.detailed_mechanism: TCA cycle intermediates are siphoned off for biosynthesis (e.g., α-ketoglutarate → glutamate; oxaloacetate → aspartate), requiring replenishment via anaplerotic reactions: (1) PEP carboxylase: PEP + CO₂ → oxaloacetate; (2) Pyruvate carboxylase: pyruvate + CO₂ + ATP → oxaloacetate; (3) Malic enzyme: pyruvate + CO₂ + NADPH → malate + NADP⁺. The glyoxylate shunt bypasses decarboxylation steps (isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA) via isocitrate lyase (isocitrate → glyoxylate + succinate) and malate synthase (glyoxylate + acetyl-CoA → malate), enabling net conversion of two acetyl-CoA to one oxaloacetate for gluconeogenesis. This shunt is essential for growth on acetate, fatty acids, or ethanol. Regulation: Isocitrate dehydrogenase is inactivated by phosphorylation during growth on acetate, diverting flux to the shunt; cAMP-CRP activates shunt genes under carbon limitation.

examples.process: Glyoxylate Shunt in E. coli Growth on Acetate
examples.explanation: When E. coli grows on acetate as sole carbon source, acetyl-CoA enters TCA cycle but cannot yield net oxaloacetate due to decarboxylations. The glyoxylate shunt solves this: isocitrate lyase cleaves isocitrate → succinate + glyoxylate; malate synthase condenses glyoxylate + acetyl-CoA → malate. Succinate and malate replenish TCA intermediates; malate → oxaloacetate → PEP → glucose (gluconeogenesis). Regulation: AceK kinase phosphorylates/isocitrate dehydrogenase under acetate growth, reducing its activity and diverting isocitrate to isocitrate lyase. cAMP-CRP activates aceBAK operon (shunt enzymes) when glucose is absent. This metabolic rewiring enables utilization of two-carbon compounds, expanding ecological niche.

image.alt: Glyoxylate shunt bypassing TCA decarboxylations with regulatory control
image.caption: Glyoxylate shunt: Isocitrate lyase + malate synthase bypass isocitrate dehydrogenase/α-KGDH. AceK phosphorylation inactivates isocitrate dehydrogenase during acetate growth, diverting flux to shunt. Enables gluconeogenesis from acetyl-CoA.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Calculating ATP Yield from Bacterial Respiration

worked_examples.title: Determining Energy Yield from Glucose via Aerobic vs. Anaerobic Respiration
worked_examples.problem: Escherichia coli can metabolize glucose via aerobic respiration, anaerobic respiration with nitrate, or fermentation. Given: (a) Glycolysis yields 2 ATP (substrate-level) + 2 NADH per glucose; (b) Pyruvate dehydrogenase yields 1 NADH per pyruvate; (c) TCA cycle yields 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP (substrate-level) per acetyl-CoA; (d) E. coli ETC: NADH dehydrogenase (NDH-1) pumps 4 H⁺/2e⁻; cytochrome bo₃ oxidase pumps 2 H⁺/2e⁻ and consumes 2 H⁺ in periplasm for O₂ reduction; ATP synthase requires 4 H⁺/ATP; (e) For nitrate respiration: nitrate reductase does not pump protons; assume same H⁺/ATP ratio. Calculate: (1) Total ATP yield per glucose via aerobic respiration; (2) ATP yield via nitrate respiration; (3) ATP yield via mixed-acid fermentation; (4) Explain why E. coli prefers aerobic respiration when O₂ is available.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate reducing equivalents from glucose catabolism.
step_by_step.calculation: Per glucose: Glycolysis: 2 NADH; Pyruvate dehydrogenase (2 pyruvate): 2 NADH; TCA cycle (2 acetyl-CoA): 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂. Total: 10 NADH + 2 FADH₂. Substrate-level ATP: glycolysis 2 ATP + TCA 2 ATP = 4 ATP.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate proton motive force and ATP from aerobic respiration.
step_by_step.calculation: NADH → NDH-1 (4 H⁺) → ubiquinone → bo₃ oxidase (2 H⁺ pumped, 2 H⁺ consumed in periplasm) → net 4 H⁺/NADH. FADH₂ enters at ubiquinone → bo₃ oxidase: 2 H⁺ pumped, 2 H⁺ consumed → net 0 H⁺/FADH₂ (conservative estimate; some models assign 2 H⁺). Total H⁺: (10 NADH × 4 H⁺) + (2 FADH₂ × 0 H⁺) = 40 H⁺. ATP from oxidative phosphorylation: 40 H⁺ ÷ 4 H⁺/ATP = 10 ATP. Total ATP: substrate-level 4 + oxidative 10 = 14 ATP. (Note: Traditional values cite ~38 ATP; modern estimates accounting for transport costs, proton leakage, and variable H⁺/ATP ratios yield 12-18 ATP.)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate ATP yield for nitrate respiration and fermentation.
step_by_step.calculation: Nitrate respiration: Same reducing equivalents (10 NADH + 2 FADH₂). Nitrate reductase does not pump protons; assume electrons flow through NDH-1 (4 H⁺/NADH) but terminal step yields no additional H⁺. Total H⁺: 10 NADH × 4 H⁺ = 40 H⁺ (same as aerobic if only NDH-1 contributes). ATP: 40 H⁺ ÷ 4 = 10 ATP + 4 substrate-level = 14 ATP. However, nitrate reductase may have lower efficiency; realistic yield ~10-12 ATP. Fermentation: No ETC; only substrate-level phosphorylation: glycolysis 2 ATP + variable from acetate production (1 ATP/acetyl-CoA via phosphotransacetylase/acetate kinase). Max fermentation yield: ~4-5 ATP/glucose.

Aerobic respiration yields ~14 ATP/glucose; fermentation yields ~4-5 ATP. Higher ATP yield supports faster growth (more biomass per glucose). Additionally, aerobic respiration fully oxidizes glucose to CO₂, minimizing waste products; fermentation produces acidic end-products (lactate, acetate) that lower pH and inhibit growth. Thus, when O₂ is available, E. coli uses aerobic respiration to maximize growth rate and fitness.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Aerobic respiration: ~14 ATP/glucose (modern estimate). (2) Nitrate respiration: ~10-14 ATP/glucose (depending on ETC efficiency). (3) Fermentation: ~4-5 ATP/glucose. (4) Preference for aerobic respiration: Higher ATP yield supports faster growth; complete oxidation minimizes inhibitory waste products. This demonstrates how energy yield shapes metabolic strategy: bacteria prioritize high-yield pathways when conditions permit, switching to lower-yield alternatives only when necessary.
worked_examples.concept_applied: ATP yield calculations integrate substrate-level phosphorylation (glycolysis, TCA) and oxidative phosphorylation (ETC proton pumping, ATP synthase). Modern estimates account for variable H⁺/ATP ratios, transport costs, and proton leakage, yielding lower values than traditional textbook numbers. Understanding energy yields explains metabolic preferences and ecological strategies: high-yield respiration supports rapid growth; fermentation enables survival under anoxia despite lower efficiency.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Glucose Catabolism to Energy Generation
sections.introduction: Bacterial glucose catabolism follows a logical sequence from uptake and phosphorylation through central pathways to terminal energy-generating processes. Understanding this flow reveals how bacteria extract maximum energy from nutrients under varying conditions.

process_flow.description: Glucose is transported into the cell and phosphorylated to trap it intracellularly and prime it for catabolism.
process_flow.mechanism: Transport systems: (1) PTS (phosphotransferase system): Glucose + PEP → glucose-6-P + pyruvate; couples transport to phosphorylation, conserving ATP; (2) ABC transporters: Glucose + ATP → glucose-6-P + ADP + Pi; high-affinity, used under low glucose. Phosphorylation prevents efflux and activates glucose for glycolysis. PTS also regulates catabolite repression via phosphorylation state of EIIA⁽ᴳˡᶜ⁾.
process_flow.result: Intracellular glucose-6-phosphate; ready for entry into glycolysis, pentose phosphate, or glycogen synthesis
applications: PTS links transport to regulation (catabolite repression)
ABC transporters enable scavenging under nutrient limitation

image.alt: Bacterial glucose transport systems: PTS and ABC transporter
image.caption: Glucose uptake: PTS (PEP-dependent, regulatory) or ABC transporter (ATP-dependent, high-affinity). Phosphorylation to glucose-6-P traps glucose and primes for catabolism.

process_flow.process: Central Pathway Flux: Glycolysis, PPP, or ED
process_flow.description: Glucose-6-phosphate is catabolized via glycolysis (EMP), pentose phosphate pathway, or Entner-Doudoroff pathway based on metabolic demands.
process_flow.mechanism: Pathway choice: (1) Glycolysis (EMP): Maximal ATP yield; regulated at phosphofructokinase (inhibited by ATP, citrate); (2) Pentose phosphate: NADPH and pentose production; oxidative branch regulated by NADP⁺/NADPH ratio; (3) ED pathway: Common in Pseudomonas; lower ATP but direct NADPH. Flux partitioning: Cells may use multiple pathways simultaneously; e.g., 70% glycolysis + 30% PPP balances ATP and NADPH needs. Intermediates feed biosynthesis: glyceraldehyde-3-P → lipids; 3-PGA → serine; PEP → aromatic amino acids.
process_flow.result: Pyruvate, NAD(P)H, ATP, and biosynthetic precursors produced; carbon flux matched to cellular demands
applications: Pathway choice reflects ecological strategy (fast growth vs. stress tolerance)
Precursor supply links catabolism to anabolism
Regulation prevents futile cycles and waste

image.alt: Flux partitioning among glycolysis, pentose phosphate, and ED pathways
image.caption: Central pathways: Glycolysis (ATP maximization), PPP (NADPH/pentoses), ED (NADPH + lower ATP). Flux partitioning regulated by allosteric effectors (ATP, NADPH) and transcriptional control (cAMP-CRP).

process_flow.process: Terminal Energy Generation: Respiration or Fermentation
process_flow.description: Pyruvate is oxidized via TCA cycle and ETC (respiration) or fermented to regenerate NAD⁺, depending on electron acceptor availability.
process_flow.mechanism: Respiration: Pyruvate → acetyl-CoA (PDH) → TCA cycle → NADH/FADH₂ → ETC → proton motive force → ATP synthase. Terminal acceptor determines pathway: O₂ (aerobic), NO₃⁻ (denitrification), SO₄²⁻ (sulfate reduction), etc. Fermentation: Pyruvate → lactate, ethanol, acetate, etc., via substrate-level phosphorylation; no ETC, lower ATP yield. Regulation: ArcAB two-component system represses TCA/ETC genes under anaerobiosis; Fnr activates anaerobic respiration genes. Switching is rapid (minutes) to match environmental O₂.
process_flow.result: ATP generated via oxidative phosphorylation (respiration) or substrate-level phosphorylation (fermentation); NAD⁺ regenerated for continued glycolysis
applications: Respiration maximizes ATP yield for rapid growth
Regulatory switching optimizes fitness across conditions

image.alt: Respiration vs. fermentation pathways from pyruvate
image.caption: Terminal metabolism: Respiration (TCA + ETC → high ATP) requires terminal acceptor (O₂, NO₃⁻, etc.); Fermentation (substrate-level phosphorylation → low ATP) regenerates NAD⁺ without acceptor. ArcAB/Fnr regulate switching based on O₂.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Metabolic Engineering and Synthetic Pathways
sections.definition: Bacterial metabolic versatility enables engineering of novel pathways for biotechnology, including biofuel production, chemical synthesis, and bioremediation, by rewiring native metabolism or introducing heterologous enzymes.

process_types.energy_profile: Redirects carbon flux from biomass to target products (ethanol, butanol, fatty acids), often at cost to growth rate
process_types.direction: Native metabolism → pathway modification (enzyme overexpression, knockout of competing routes) → enhanced product yield
process_types.function: Convert renewable feedstocks (glucose, cellulose, CO₂) into fuels and chemicals using engineered bacterial chassis
goals: Maximize carbon yield to target product
Balance redox cofactors (NADH/NADPH) for pathway efficiency
Minimize byproduct formation and metabolic burden
Ensure strain robustness under industrial conditions
process_types.energy_source: Substrate catabolism (glycolysis, TCA) provides ATP and reducing power for product synthesis; product formation may consume ATP/NAD(P)H

primary_example.process: Engineering E. coli for Isobutanol Production
primary_example.explanation: Isobutanol is a superior biofuel (higher energy density, lower hygroscopicity than ethanol). Native E. coli does not produce isobutanol; pathway engineered from valine biosynthesis: (1) Overexpress acetohydroxyacid synthase (IlvIH) and ketol-acid reductoisomerase (IlvC) to convert pyruvate → 2-ketoisovalerate; (2) Introduce heterologous 2-ketoisovalerate decarboxylase (Kivd from Lactococcus) → isobutyraldehyde; (3) Express alcohol dehydrogenase (AdhA) → isobutanol. Challenges: (1) Competing pathways (valine synthesis, lactate fermentation) divert carbon; solved by deleting ldhA, ilvE; (2) NADPH demand for IlvC; addressed by transhydrogenase overexpression or NADPH-dependent enzyme engineering; (3) Product toxicity; mitigated by in situ extraction or tolerance engineering. Result: Engineered strains produce >50 g/L isobutanol, demonstrating metabolic engineering potential.

image.alt: Engineered isobutanol pathway in E. coli with competing route knockouts
image.caption: Isobutanol engineering: Pyruvate → 2-ketoisovalerate (IlvIH, IlvC) → isobutyraldehyde (Kivd) → isobutanol (AdhA). Knockouts (ldhA, ilvE) block competing routes. NADPH balancing critical for yield.

image.alt: General strategies for metabolic engineering: enzyme overexpression, pathway knockout, cofactor balancing
image.caption: Engineering strategies: Overexpress pathway enzymes; delete competing routes; balance cofactors (NADH/NADPH); enhance product tolerance. Iterative design-build-test cycles optimize yield and robustness.

process_types.type: Synthetic Autotrophy: Engineering CO₂ Fixation into Heterotrophs
process_types.energy_profile: High energy cost: CO₂ fixation requires ATP and reducing power; engineered strains often grow slower than native heterotrophs
process_types.direction: Heterotrophic chassis (E. coli, yeast) + autotrophic modules (Calvin cycle, reductive TCA) → CO₂ utilization
process_types.function: Enable industrial microbes to fix CO₂, reducing feedstock costs and carbon footprint for bioproduction
goals: Integrate functional CO₂ fixation pathway into heterotrophic metabolism
Balance energy/redox demands of fixation with host metabolism
Achieve net carbon gain from CO₂ under controlled conditions
Scale to industrial bioreactors with gas-liquid mass transfer optimization
process_types.energy_source: Light (if phototrophic modules added) or organic substrates (mixotrophy) provide ATP/NADPH for CO₂ fixation; ultimate goal is autotrophic growth on CO₂ + H₂ or formate

primary_example.process: Engineering Calvin Cycle into E. coli for CO₂ Fixation
primary_example.explanation: Native E. coli cannot fix CO₂; engineered strain expresses Calvin cycle enzymes: (1) Rubisco (rbcL/S from Cyanobacteria) + Rubisco activase; (2) Phosphoribulokinase (PRK); (3) Supporting enzymes (transketolase, aldolase) already present. Challenges: (1) Rubisco oxygenation (photorespiration); addressed by high CO₂ conditions or engineering Rubisco specificity; (2) ATP/NADPH demand: Calvin cycle requires 3 ATP + 2 NADPH per CO₂; met by overexpressing transhydrogenase (NADH → NADPH) and optimizing respiration; (3) Toxic intermediates (e.g., glycolaldehyde from Rubisco side reactions); mitigated by pathway balancing. Result: Engineered E. coli incorporates ¹³CO₂ into biomass, demonstrating functional CO₂ fixation. While not yet autotrophic, this platform enables mixotrophic production of chemicals from CO₂ + organic co-substrates, reducing carbon footprint.

image.alt: Engineered Calvin cycle in E. coli with supporting modifications for CO₂ fixation
image.caption: Calvin cycle engineering: Express Rubisco + PRK in E. coli; balance ATP/NADPH via transhydrogenase; suppress photorespiration via high CO₂. Enables ¹³CO₂ incorporation into biomass; step toward autotrophic bioproduction.

image.alt: Comparison of synthetic autotrophy approaches: Calvin cycle, reductive TCA, Wood-Ljungdahl pathway
image.caption: Synthetic autotrophy: Calvin cycle (high ATP cost, O₂-sensitive Rubisco), reductive TCA (O₂-sensitive enzymes), Wood-Ljungdahl (low ATP, strict anaerobiosis). Choice depends on host physiology and process conditions.

sections.relationship: Metabolic engineering and synthetic autotrophy represent applied extensions of natural metabolic diversity: engineering redirects native pathways for product synthesis; synthetic autotrophy introduces novel pathways to expand substrate range. Both rely on deep understanding of bacterial metabolism—pathway kinetics, regulation, and integration—to achieve predictable, scalable outcomes.

sections.section_title: Molecular Mechanisms: Redox Balancing and Cofactor Regeneration
sections.purpose: Bacterial metabolism requires precise balancing of redox cofactors (NAD⁺/NADH, NADP⁺/NADPH) to sustain catabolism, biosynthesis, and stress responses; dedicated mechanisms regenerate oxidized/reduced forms as needed.

activation.definition: Redox balancing involves enzymatic interconversion of cofactor pools (transhydrogenases), pathway selection (NADH- vs. NADPH-generating routes), and regulatory adjustments to match supply with demand.
activation.effect: Imbalances disrupt metabolism: excess NADH inhibits glycolysis/TCA; NADPH shortage limits biosynthesis and antioxidant defense. Bacteria maintain homeostasis via transhydrogenases (PntAB: NADH + NADP⁺ → NAD⁺ + NADPH, proton-pumping; UdhA: reversible, soluble), pathway branching (e.g., ED pathway generates NADPH directly), and transcriptional control (e.g., SoxRS induces NADPH-generating enzymes under oxidative stress).

image.alt: Redox balancing via transhydrogenases, pathway selection, and regulatory control
image.caption: Redox homeostasis: Transhydrogenases (PntAB, UdhA) interconvert NAD(H)/NADP(H); pathway choice (EMP vs. ED) adjusts cofactor yields; regulators (SoxRS, OxyR) induce NADPH-generating enzymes under stress.

key_properties.explanation: Transhydrogenases catalyze reversible hydride transfer between NAD(H) and NADP(H), balancing redox pools. Two types: (1) Membrane-bound PntAB: Proton-pumping, favors NADPH production (NADH + NADP⁺ + H⁺_out → NAD⁺ + NADPH + H⁺_in); couples cofactor balance to proton motive force; (2) Soluble UdhA: Reversible, no proton coupling; favors NADH production under some conditions. PntAB is critical for biosynthesis: NADPH demand for fatty acid/amino acid synthesis exceeds glycolytic/PPP supply; PntAB converts excess NADH (from respiration) to NADPH. Deletion of pntAB reduces growth on acetate (high NADPH demand) but not glucose. Engineering PntAB overexpression improves yields of NADPH-dependent products (e.g., isobutanol, polyhydroxyalkanoates).

image.alt: PntAB and UdhA transhydrogenase mechanisms and physiological roles
image.caption: Transhydrogenases: PntAB (membrane, proton-pumping, favors NADPH) balances respiration-derived NADH with biosynthetic NADPH demand; UdhA (soluble, reversible) provides flexibility. Critical for redox homeostasis in diverse conditions.

key_properties.property: NADPH-Generating Pathways for Biosynthesis and Stress Defense
key_properties.explanation: NADPH is essential for reductive biosynthesis (fatty acids, nucleotides) and antioxidant systems (glutathione, thioredoxin). Bacteria employ multiple NADPH sources: (1) Pentose phosphate pathway (oxidative branch): Glucose-6-P dehydrogenase (Zwf) and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase (Gnd) generate 2 NADPH per glucose-6-P; (2) Isocitrate dehydrogenase (NADP⁺-dependent): In some bacteria (e.g., Corynebacterium), IDH uses NADP⁺, linking TCA cycle to NADPH supply; (3) Malic enzyme: Pyruvate + CO₂ + NADPH → malate + NADP⁺ (reversible); (4) Ferredoxin-NADP⁺ reductase: In phototrophs, light-driven ferredoxin reduction powers NADPH synthesis. Regulation: zwf and gnd are induced by NADP⁺ (via transcription factors like Zur); oxidative stress (H₂O₂) activates OxyR/SoxRS, upregulating NADPH-generating enzymes to replenish glutathione/thioredoxin. This redundancy ensures NADPH supply across conditions.

image.alt: Major NADPH-generating pathways in bacteria with regulatory inputs
image.caption: NADPH sources: PPP oxidative branch (Zwf, Gnd), NADP⁺-IDH, malic enzyme, ferredoxin-NADP⁺ reductase (phototrophs). Regulated by NADP⁺/NADPH ratio and oxidative stress sensors (OxyR, SoxRS).

key_properties.explanation: Bacteria use redox-sensitive transcription factors to adjust metabolism to cellular redox state. Key regulators: (1) ArcAB: Two-component system; ArcB kinase autophosphorylates under anaerobiosis (reduced quinones), phosphorylates ArcA, which represses TCA/ETC genes and activates fermentation genes; (2) Fnr: O₂-sensitive [4Fe-4S] cluster; anaerobic Fnr activates anaerobic respiration/fermentation genes; (3) SoxRS: Superoxide oxidizes [2Fe-2S] in SoxR, activating SoxS transcription, which induces NADPH-generating enzymes (zwf, fpr) and antioxidant defenses; (4) OxyR: H₂O₂ oxidizes cysteines, activating transcription of catalase (katG), glutathione reductase (gor), and NADPH-generating genes. These regulators create feedback loops: e.g., oxidative stress → SoxRS activation → NADPH production → glutathione reduction → stress mitigation. Understanding redox regulation enables engineering of strains with enhanced stress tolerance or product yields.

image.alt: Redox-sensitive regulators (ArcAB, Fnr, SoxRS, OxyR) and their target pathways
image.caption: Redox regulation: ArcAB (quinone redox state) controls aerobic/anaerobic metabolism; Fnr (O₂) regulates anaerobic respiration; SoxRS/OxyR (ROS) induce antioxidant/NADPH pathways. Integrated network maintains redox homeostasis.

summary: Bacterial metabolism encompasses diverse strategies for energy generation and carbon acquisition, classified by energy source (light/chemical) and carbon source (CO₂/organic). Phototrophs use light for ATP synthesis: photoautotrophs (Cyanobacteria) fix CO₂ via Calvin cycle; photoheterotrophs use organic carbon. Chemotrophs oxidize chemicals: chemoautotrophs (nitrifiers, sulfur oxidizers) use inorganic donors + CO₂; chemoheterotrophs (most pathogens, decomposers) use organic compounds for both energy and carbon. Energy generation occurs via respiration (aerobic/anaerobic electron transport chains generating proton motive force) or fermentation (substrate-level phosphorylation without ETC). Central pathways—glycolysis (EMP), pentose phosphate, TCA cycle—provide ATP, reducing power, and biosynthetic precursors, with flux regulated by allosteric effectors, covalent modification, and transcriptional control (e.g., catabolite repression, ArcAB, Fnr). Metabolic flexibility enables adaptation: E. coli switches from aerobic respiration to fermentation based on O₂ availability. Advanced applications include metabolic engineering for biofuels (isobutanol production) and synthetic autotrophy (engineering CO₂ fixation into heterotrophs). Understanding bacterial metabolism is essential for ecology (nutrient cycling), medicine (targeting pathogen metabolism), and biotechnology (strain engineering for sustainable production).
